response: The internet’s origins trace to the 1960s, when the US Department of Defense funded networking research, leading to ARPANET, first demonstrated in 1969 with a message from UCLA to Stanford Research Institute. Growth continued through the 1970s, and in 1983 the shift to TCP/IP protocols created the foundation of today’s internet, followed by Tim Berners-Lee’s invention of the World Wide Web in 1989 at CERN (with HTML, URLs, and HTTP) and Mosaic’s 1993 release that made the web widely usable. The late 1990s dot-com boom fueled major investment but many firms failed in the 2001 crash, while survivors such as Amazon and Google became major companies. Later developments—social media in the mid-2000s, mobile internet after the 2007 iPhone, and cloud computing with AWS’s 2006 launch—helped make the internet central to modern life, now connecting over 5 billion people.
